meta.title: E64 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 6 Series E64 engine — N52, N62, M57, S85 V10. Real UK costs, reliability data and common failures. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series E64 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E64 engine typically costs £2,500–£15,000 supply only, depending on engine — the N52 six-cylinder sits at £2,500–£5,000, while the N62 V8 runs £6,000–£10,000 and the S85 V10 can exceed £15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series E64 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 (630i, 2004–2007) — BMW's last naturally aspirated straight-six without direct injection. It delivers smooth, linear power and is the most reliable engine in the E64 range. It's the one we recommend for daily use.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series E64?

acceptedAnswer.text: N62 V8 valve stem seal failure (blue smoke on startup), N62 V8 cooling system failures (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank), S85 V10 rod bearing wear (M6), and — unrelated to the engine — the convertible roof mechanism hydraulic system failures.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 6 Series E64 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N52 straight-six is very reliable. The N62 V8 is a glorious engine but has well-documented valve-stem-seal and cooling system issues — budget for these repairs. The S85 V10 in the M6 is spectacular but demands a significant maintenance budget. The 635d diesel is robust but rare.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series E64 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N62 V8 (645Ci/650i) is not an 'avoid' engine — it's an 'understand what you're getting into' engine. The valve-stem-seal issue is well-documented and expensive to fix. The S85 V10 is for collectors only — maintenance costs are substantial. If you want the most trouble-free E64, buy a 630i with the N52.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E64 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Blue smoke on startup (N62 valve stem seals), coolant loss or overheating (N62, N52), metallic knocking from the bottom end (S85 rod bearings), or persistent check engine lights are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series E64 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £18,000 E64, a £6,500 N62 recon is ~36% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £9,000 example, the same recon brushes our 70% BMW threshold — walk away. Also factor in the condition of the convertible roof mechanism — this can be a significant additional cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 6 Series E64 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 straight-six can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The N62 V8's lifespan depends on valve-stem-seal and cooling system maintenance — with proper care, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The S85 V10 is the most fragile — rod bearings are the limiting factor.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E64 and the E63?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E64 is the convertible body of the second-generation 6 Series (2004–2010, this page), while the E63 is the coupé. Both share the same platform, engines, and interior, but the E64 has additional structural reinforcement and a complex hydraulic roof mechanism. The E64 is heavier and more expensive to maintain — see the E63 Generation Page for that model's specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series E64 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on N52/N53/N62/M57/S85 units. Tell us your variant (630i, 645Ci, 650i, 635d, M6) and year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 6 Series E64 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E64 convertible — from the 3.0-litre N52 straight-six to the N62 V8 and the S85 V10 that powered the M6. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the N62 valve-stem-seal issues that still haunt high-mileage V8 cars. Backed by 256+ real UK E64 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 6 Series E64 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E64 convertible — from the entry-level 630i six-cylinder to the range-topping M6 V10.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All reliability ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any E64 engine family. Ratings are derived from cross-referencing specialist and owner sources. The N52 straight-six is widely considered the most reliable option. The M57 diesel (635d) is robust but rare in convertible form. The N62 V8 has well-documented valve-stem-seal and cooling system issues. The S85 V10 in the M6 is spectacular but demands a significant maintenance budget.

overview.intro: The E64 is the convertible body of BMW's second-generation 6 Series, launched in May 2004 as the direct successor to the E24 6 Series after a 14-year hiatus. It shared the E63 coupé's controversial Bangle-designed bodywork but added a fully automatic soft-top roof that could be operated at speeds up to 20 mph. The engine range spanned from the 3.0-litre N52 straight-six to the 4.4-litre and 4.8-litre N62 V8s, a late-arriving twin-turbo 3.0-litre diesel, and the S85 V10 in the M6. A 2007 Life Cycle Impulse (LCI) brought revised styling, the 635d diesel, and the replacement of the N52 with the N53 in the 630i.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E63/E64 (shared with E63 Coupé) • Predecessor: BMW 6 Series E24 (1976–1989) • Successor: BMW 6 Series F12 Convertible (2011–2018) • Sibling body styles in this generation: E63 Coupé (2-door)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E64 convertible generated 256 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the N62 V8 petrol units as these grand tourers age into enthusiast ownership and maintenance cycles.

items.quote: The 635d is the rarest and most surprising E64 engine — a 3.0-litre twin-turbo diesel with 286 bhp and 580 Nm of torque that transforms the convertible into a genuine continent-crushing grand tourer. It's the only E64 engine that delivers effortless torque and 40+ MPG economy. The M57 is one of BMW's most durable diesel engines.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the ultimate E64 cruiser with diesel economy.

items.quote: The N52 is BMW's last naturally aspirated straight-six without direct injection — smooth, linear power delivery, and none of the N62's V8 complexity or the N53's high-pressure injector issues. It's the most reliable E64 engine and the one we recommend for daily use. The 258 bhp is plenty for a grand tourer.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want straight-six refinement and proven reliability.

items.quote: The N62 V8 is a glorious engine — 367 bhp, 490 Nm of torque, and a soundtrack that defines the E64 experience. But it suffers from well-documented valve-stem-seal failure (blue smoke on startup), cooling system failures, alternator bracket gasket oil leaks, and PCV system issues. It's the most expensive non-M E64 engine to maintain.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 character and are prepared for V8 maintenance costs.

items.quote: The S85 V10 is one of BMW's most spectacular engines — 507 bhp, 8,250 rpm redline, and a sound that's pure motorsport. But it's also one of the most expensive to maintain: rod bearing wear is the defining failure, requiring an engine-out service at £3,000–£5,000. Throttle actuators, VANOS and SMG pumps are additional common failures. A full engine replacement can exceed £15,000. Only 5,000 E64 M6 convertibles were built.
items.whoItsFor: collectors with deep pockets who understand the S85's demands.

items.quote: The 630i delivers 258 bhp, 300 Nm of torque, and genuine 30+ MPG economy in a grand tourer package. It's the lightest E64 engine, the most reliable, and the cheapest to maintain. It's the sensible choice that still delivers the E64 experience — just without the V8's running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the E64 experience without the V8's fuel and maintenance bills.

items.quote: The 635d is the rarest E64 engine — only produced from 2007 to 2010 and in limited numbers. It's often overlooked in favour of the V8s, but it delivers 286 bhp, 580 Nm of torque, and genuine 40+ MPG. It's the E64 for buyers who actually drive their cars.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the ultimate E64 GT car for long-distance touring.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — reliable, simple, no turbo worries

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals, cooling system, PCV
rows.ownershipVerdict: Most Expensive Non-M — more power, same issues
Best Diesel — bulletproof M57, rare in convertible

economicsRule.title: The E64 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,500–£10,000 N62 recon against a £8,000–£20,000 E64 sits at 33–125% of the car's value. On a sub-£10,000 E64, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £18,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 36–56% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first, and always check for valve-stem-seal smoke on V8 cars. The convertible roof mechanism also adds a unique cost dimension to E64 ownership.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E64 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 630i (N52, pre-LCI) — simple straight-six, reliable, cheap parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E64 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M6 (S85 V10) — rod bearings, VANOS, throttle actuators; V10 running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E64 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 630i (N52) — the most reliable E64 engine, the cheapest to run, and still a genuine grand tourer.

problems.title: N62 V8 Valve Stem Seal Failure
problems.rootCause: The N62 V8 suffers from valve stem seals that harden and lose their sealing ability over time. This allows oil to seep past the seals and into the combustion chamber, particularly when the car has been sitting overnight. The classic symptom is a puff of blue smoke on cold startup. Over time, oil consumption increases and catalytic converters can be damaged.
problems.recommendation: If your N62 V8 smokes on startup, budget for valve-stem-seal replacement immediately — a £1,500–£2,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows if the catalytic converters are destroyed or the engine suffers further damage. Use high-quality oil and change it regularly to slow seal degradation.

cta.label: Compare N62 engine prices for your E64 645Ci/650i →

problems.title: N62 V8 Cooling System Failures
problems.rootCause: The N62 V8's cooling system is a known weak point — the water pump, thermostat, expansion tank and plastic coolant pipes all age poorly. The engine's aluminium block is intolerant of overheating, and a failed cooling system can lead to catastrophic engine failure. The alternator bracket gasket is also prone to oil leaks, which can drip onto the exhaust and cause smoke.
problems.recommendation: If you own an N62 V8 E64, treat the cooling system as a consumable — replace the water pump, thermostat and expansion tank proactively at 80,000 miles. A £400–£900 preventative service is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows an overheat.

cta.label: Compare N62 engine prices for your E64 645Ci/650i →

problems.title: S85 V10 Rod Bearing Failure
problems.rootCause: The S85 V10 is notorious for rod bearing wear — the bearings can fail due to insufficient oil clearance from the factory, leading to catastrophic engine failure. The classic symptom is a metallic knocking sound from the bottom end, particularly when cold. This is the single most expensive failure on the E64 M6.
problems.recommendation: If you own an E64 M6, budget for rod bearing replacement as preventative maintenance at 50,000 miles — £2,500–£4,000 is dramatically cheaper than the £8,000+ replacement that follows if the bearings fail. Listen for any metallic knocking and use the correct 10W-60 oil.

cta.label: Compare S85 engine prices for your E64 M6 →

problems.title: E64 Convertible Roof Mechanism Issues
problems.rootCause: The E64's fully automatic soft-top roof relies on a complex hydraulic system with multiple rams, pumps and sensors. Common issues include hydraulic fluid leaks, failing roof motors, sensor failures, and worn roof latches. The roof can become stuck partially open or fail to operate entirely.
problems.recommendation: Have the roof mechanism inspected regularly — a £200–£500 preventative service is dramatically cheaper than the £1,200+ repair that follows if the hydraulic system fails. Check for any hesitation or unusual noises when operating the roof.

cta.label: Compare E64 roof mechanism repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: E64 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E64 convertible only — the two-door, four-seat convertible produced from 2004 to 2010. E63 Coupé shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, as fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The 645Ci was produced only from 2004–2005 and replaced by the 650i from October 2005. The 635d was introduced with the 2007 LCI.
